message:
Last weekend I was digging through a drawer looking for batteries (very on-brand for me) and found a stack of old board games my kids had basically forgotten about. We played for *hours* with zero screens, zero complaints, and I realized something: if we ever lost power, we'd actually be fine because we already know how to entertain ourselves the analog way.

It got me thinking about preparedness differently. So much of it is about the practical stuff—the water, the documents, the go-bag checklist—but I think there's something quietly powerful about having hobbies and activities that don't need electricity. Card games, books, puzzles, even just knowing you can cook outdoors. It's not just a backup plan; it's honestly made our family time better *now*.

A little refresher on what real readiness looks like (beyond the obvious) helped me see the bigger picture.

What low-tech activities does your family actually enjoy? Are those things tucked away, or part of your regular rotation?
